rationale_for_baseline_tier: Active upper-gastrointestinal haemorrhage — coffee-ground haematemesis with melaena — on a background of probable advanced gastric malignancy, accompanied by haemodynamic instability (clinician-measured BP 95/60, HR 112) and patient-reportable profound weakness and lightheadedness. This is early haemorrhagic shock with an imminent risk of exsanguination, so T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. The T4/T5 ceiling is legitimately breached here because the patient/carer can themselves report genuine time-critical emergency features — vomiting dark 'coffee-ground' material, black tarry stools, and near-collapse weakness — rather than the tier resting only on the in-person abdominal exam and bloods.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fresh bright-red haematemesis, large-volume vomiting of blood, or ongoing dark vomiting
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call/stay on the line with 000 now
escalation_edges.rationale: Fresh or large-volume haematemesis signals brisk ongoing bleeding and impending decompensation; emergency ambulance and resuscitation cannot wait.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he vomits fresh red blood or a large amount, tell the 000 dispatcher straight away and keep him lying flat.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Collapse, fainting, or loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: Collapse indicates decompensated hypovolaemic shock; lay him flat with legs raised, keep him warm, and be ready for CPR guidance from the dispatcher.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he faints or becomes unresponsive, lay him flat and raise his legs, keep him warm, tell 000 immediately, and follow their instructions — begin CPR if he stops breathing normally and they guide you.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline — active coffee-ground vomiting with profound weakness in a patient with weight loss and PPI-refractory pain
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — do not wait for clinic hours
escalation_edges.rationale: The presenting picture itself is already a T5 emergency; the alarm features additionally mandate urgent inpatient endoscopy and specialist referral, but the immediate action is an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Given the dark vomiting and how weak he is, he needs an ambulance right now — call 000 and tell them he may be bleeding from his stomach.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult (~68), frail, cachectic and bedbound
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reduced physiological reserve means shock can decompensate quickly and a 'compensated' blood pressure can drop abruptly — keep the threshold for escalation low and do not be reassured by an initially near-normal appearance.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adult child present at the bedside and making the call
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct the carer to keep the patient lying flat, take nothing by mouth, watch for fresh blood or collapse, unlock the door for paramedics, and relay changes to 000 — the carer is the hands at the scene.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he may be bleeding from his stomach and is very weak. Keep him lying flat and do not let him get up or walk. Give him nothing to eat or drink. Stay with him, watch for fresh red blood in the vomit or any fainting, and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the hospital with the five-month history of PPI-refractory epigastric pain, ~12 kg unintentional weight loss and early satiety, today's coffee-ground haematemesis and dark stools, and the home esomeprazole. The admitting team will arrange urgent endoscopy, bloods and staging; the GP should receive the discharge summary and endoscopy/biopsy results for ongoing oncology coordination.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats the presentation as chronic dyspepsia or a routine/2-week-wait referral and misses the active upper-GI bleed with early shock.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Ongoing haemorrhage progresses to decompensated hypovolaemic shock and death while the patient waits at home.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognises a serious problem but advises self-transport to ED rather than an emergency ambulance for a haemodynamically unstable bleed.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is the correct tier here; there is no meaningful over-triage risk for an active bleed with shock signs.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot confirm the source of the bleed or the cancer diagnosis, and cannot measure the true blood pressure or haemoglobin.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the recognisable emergency (active upper-GI bleed with shock proxies) and escalate to 000; flag the malignancy alarm features as requiring urgent endoscopy — do not attempt to confirm or exclude cancer remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using diagnostic uncertainty to justify waiting, reassurance, or a routine referral — 'it might just be his chronic reflux' — instead of escalating the emergency.
